text: We are introducing a tubeless gnosis technology in rectal sigmoid cancer. It is a fifty-six year old female with a rectal sigmoid lesion shown in colonoscopy. Pathology confirmed that it is an adenocarcinoma. CT scan shows this is a localized disease with a distant metastasis. Since the lesion is small, we use carbon nanoparticle to track the mass, which is showing blue. Here is the operation procedure. The abdominal cavity exhalation finds there is no liver or omentum metastasis.

The surgeon identifies the primary tumor located at the junction of the rectum and sigmoid colon. The initial step involves mobilizing the sigmoid colon by following the plane between the Toldt's fascia and mesocolon, ensuring clear distal and proximal margins.

The surgeon dissects lymph nodes and soft tissues along the inferior mesenteric artery, expanding the plane to free the sigmoid colon. The left ureter is identified and preserved. The distal rectum is skeletonized, and the rectal lumen is closed at the distal margin by ligation. The surgeon washes the distal rectum from the anus. The specimen is amputated from the proximal colon using Ethicon and OGIA. After washing, the distal rectal lumen is clean, and the bowel is cut using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon inserts the stapler anvil into the abdominal cavity through the rectal lumen via the anus. After separating the bowel wall with a sterilized film, the specimen is extracted from the anus and removed from the body. The distal lumen is closed using GAA, and the area is washed multiple times to reduce contamination risk. The anvil is then placed into the proximal lumen to complete the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a bubble test on the anastomosis, confirming no air leakage. The mesocolon hiatus is closed with sutures, followed by closure of the trochanter incisions. The procedure concludes with the patient having no tubes post-surgery.

The duration of the surgery is about two hours and the blood loss is less than fifteen milliliters. The patient can take some liquid right after the surgery and walk around the following day. Bowel movement recovers at the second day and patient is discharged at the fifth day after surgery without complications. Thanks for your attention.
